fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

WILSON J

INTRODUCTION

These reasons explain my ruling[1] for the evidence of four medical expert witnesses in this parenting case to give their evidence concurrently, or, to use the modern idiom, “in the hot tub”.
Very little in the way of jurisprudence in this court[2] provides guidance in relation to when and in what circumstances it is appropriate for experts to give their evidence concurrently, as opposed to sequentially as is more usual.
Two useful articles then written by sitting justices of the Federal Court of Australia provide helpful insights.[3]

THE ISSUE IN CONTEXT

This parenting proceeding was commenced in 2022 in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (“FCFCOA”). The proceeding was eventually transferred to Division 1 of the FCFCOA and entered in my docket. It was fixed for trial twice in 2025. The trial fixed for 10 February 2025 with an estimated duration of seven days was vacated by consent inter alia for the father to attend upon Dr Paul Foulkes for an updated assessment and report and for the parties and child to attend upon Ms Joy Slattery for the purpose of a further updated family report. Further, the trial fixed to commence on 28 July 2025 with an estimated duration of five days was vacated as the court was advised that the estimate of the likely duration of the trial provided to the court was unrealistic. Ultimately the case was refixed for trial to commence on 2 March 2026 with an estimated duration of 10 days.
Extensive allegations of family violence were made by the mother against the father. He denied those allegations. It was also alleged that the father suffered from a variety of psychological conditions.
Four medical witnesses provided expert reports, three psychologists and one psychiatrist. Certain psychological evidence was to the effect that the father, over eight years ago, suffered from certain behavioural traits rendering him aggressive at that time, since which (with regular treatment) he has overcome that aggressive behaviour. A third psychologist provided a report in which she expressed the opinion that the father’s behaviour was unlikely to change and that stressors beyond his control were likely to precipitate an adverse reaction in the father leading to aggressive behaviour thereby creating an atmosphere of fear in the mother.
Prior to the commencement of the trial, counsel for the father prepared and circulated what he called a trial plan, plotting a sequence of witnesses over the anticipated 10 days of trial. No counsel disagreed with the sequence of witnesses. The four expert witnesses were scheduled to give their evidence after evidence had been adduced from the father and his parents and from the mother and her parents. It was estimated that the experts would require at least two full days of court time if the conventional adversarial method of adducing expert evidence was adopted.
The time spent in cross-examination of the father of the one part and of the mother of the other part over ran the programmed duration of the evidence of each according to the parties’ trial plan.
After delivering my ruling refusing the mother’s and the ICL’s recusal application,[4] I asked counsel whether they considered the experts’ evidence was amenable to its being given concurrently, that is to say, “in the hot tub”. Each said he and she needed to take instructions. Then followed a discussion about the procedure to be adopted if it was agreed that the experts’ evidence would be given concurrently.

CONCURRENT EVIDENCE

This court has been slow to embrace the concept of concurrent evidence. Conversely, in the Federal Court of Australia concurrent evidence is commonplace. The article by the Hon Justice Rares revealed that concurrent evidence has traction in courts across the common law world.
No fixed method of the adducing of concurrent evidence enjoys universal application. That may explain why written recitals of the procedure are guarded in their expression of applicability to all circumstances. One size does not fit all. That said, in this court general principles of procedural fairness apply requiring all counsel to be given a fair opportunity to question all experts. In the specific context of a parenting case in Division 1 of the FCFCOA, strict application of the rules of evidence are relaxed (under s 102NL) and under s 102NE of the Family Law Act, the trial judge must actively direct, control and manage the conduct of the proceeding. To that must be added the overarching purpose of family law practice and procedure set out in s 95(1)(d) of the Family Law Act, which requires litigation concerning parenting to be conducted “as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible” as well as the statutory duty imposed on parties and their legal representatives to give effect to the purpose reposed in s 95.
Several days were proposed for the experts to give their evidence by conventional means. That involved one witness after the other entering the witness box, giving his and her evidence-in-chief and then being subjected to cross-examination. As with any cross-examination, once a witness is excused from giving further evidence after that witness’s evidence is completed, he or she can only be recalled by agreement or by leave if it becomes necessary to adduce further evidence from the witness. The point may assume importance with experts in respect of whose evidence a subsequent expert gives contrary evidence and the first expert wants to refute that later evidence with probative evidence not earlier thought necessary. Unless leave is given in that scenario to recall the first witness and then put to him or her the proposition advanced by the subsequent expert, the judge is left at risk of being misinformed about some aspect of the expert evidence or worse, about the totality of the expert evidence. That is wholly undesirable. It should be avoided wherever possible.
The hot tub operates in such manner as to avoid the risks associated with that scenario.
It must be kept uppermost in mind that in Division 1 of the FCFCOA, expert witnesses owe duties to the court – not to the parties. They should not be “hired guns”, nor should they be advocates for one party, nor should they be unduly partisan to one party or another. From the court’s perspective, it is important to obtain from all experts the totality of the available expert evidence on the issue before the court, so long as that evidence is given by a person capable of giving the relevant expert evidence within the contemplation of s 79 of the Evidence Act and the statements of principle from the High Court on the ambit of s 79 of the Evidence Act.[5]

THE CONCLAVE OF EXPERTS

In accordance with conventional practice concerning concurrent evidence among experts, I required them to confer prior to the date allocated for the hot tub with a view to preparing a document that recorded the key matters. The first was a distillation of the issues which the lay evidence raised in respect of which expert evidence was to be adduced. The second was the creation of a list of issues on which the experts agreed and the third was the creation of a list of issues on which the experts disagreed.
Counsel for the ICL helpfully assumed the obligations associated with the carriage of the creation of the list that set out those three issues.
The list was duly prepared following the convening of a conclave of the four experts in this case. It recorded the following –
The significance of the Father’s current mental health diagnosis to assessing the future risk that he may pose to [X].
The experts do not agree on this question at all.
They disagree as to the risk
They disagree as to what mental health diagnosis the father has, if any
They agree [Mr Ashdown] doesn’t have any current mental health diagnosis
The current risk factors and protective factors regarding the Father that the Court should take into account regarding appropriate parenting arrangements for [X].
The experts agree the protective factors regarding the Father that should be taken into account are:
The father’s relationship with David Stickels and Craig Gaunson;
The father’s relationship with [Ms B];
Craig Gaunson, Paul Foulkes and David Stickels agree a further protective factor is
The father’s relationship with his parents
Joy Slattery disagrees with this because of the changing relationship between the Father & his parents, & the history of assault
The experts disagree on the current risk factors:
They agree a risk factor is a poor co-parenting relationship;
They disagree on family violence, insight into family violence – including whether it is a risk at all
Whether the Father’s treatment has been successful in addressing or significantly ameliorating the risk the Father poses to progressing to unsupervised time.
The experts acknowledge they disagree as to the risk, and therefore this question is difficult to agree or disagree on. The experts agree there is some caution expressed by Joy Slattery and Paul Foulkes as to whether the risk is ameliorated
Craig Gauson and David Stickels agree to the premise of this question
Paul Foulkes says he does not agree with the sharp dichotomy of the question. He agrees that there has been help in reducing the risk but unclear as to whether this has “successfully” or “significantly “ reduced the risk.
Joy Slattery says if she accepts what Craig Gaunson and David Stickels have to say about the changes they have noticed, then she would agree there has been some success but she is not yet clear whether this addressed the risk as laid out in this question
Whether there should be a differentiation between the Father’s insight into and acknowledgment of his past aggressive and violent behaviours prior to meeting the Mother and his acknowledgement of disputed family violence allegations made by the Mother.
The experts felt that they were unclear what was being asked by this question and could not really agree or disagree.
David Stickels and Craig Gaunson agree that the Father has acknowledged his past aggressive and violent behaviours prior to meeting the Mother
David Stickels and Craig Gaunson indicated that the Father disputes he has committed family violence against the mother if the definition was limited to causing physical violence.
Whether the expert opinion would change as to the level of risk the Father poses to [X] if the Court were to determine the Mother’s allegations of family violence by the Father against her are proven or not proven or significant allegations are proven or not proven.
Overall, there was not agreement as to whether expert opinion would change on this basis or what that change would be
David Stickels said that if the allegations by the mother were proven that would not change his view about the risk at first, he would need to put that information to the father in their sessions
Craig Gaunson agrees with David Stickels.
Paul Foulkes said that his opinion would not change as most of his opinion is based on the forensic history in this matter, not on the mother’s allegations
Whether the Father would be able to maintain a connection with [X] with 6 supervised visits a year and would she be able to successfully identify with her paternal family.
Overall there was disagreement as to this
Paul Foulkes noted this question was outside of his qualifications/role in this matter
Joy Slattery said that a connection could be maintained but it is not an ideal arrangement. Enough to preserve some kind of familiarity. Joy Slattery that it could also be harmful for [X] to have connection to her Father (or paternal family) and may not be safe
Craig Gaunson disagreed with this – a connection could not be maintained – he believes it is not in the interests of [X]
David Stickels disagreed with this – it is not in [X’s] best interests and he does not think it is necessary – he also thinks it is harmful to [X] to grow up with limited connection to her Father or paternal family.
Whether professionally supervised contact between the Father and [X] is maintainable and in [X’s] best interests in the long term.
Joy Slattery, David Stickels and Craig Gauson all agreed that professional supervised contact was not maintainable and it was not in [X’s] best interests in the long term.
Paul Foulkes reiterated that this was outside of the scope of his role in this matter
The possible benefits or risks to [X’s] future development if the Father were to spend no time and have no communication with the Father.
Craig Gaunson and David Stickels felt that they had answered this with respect to the previous two questions
Paul Foulkes reiterated that this was outside of the scope of his role in this matter
Joy Slattery added 'The quality and nature of the relationship are not the only factors to be considered, bearing in mind risk & safety factors
Other issues
Paul Foulkes, David Stickels and Craig Gaunson agreed that if the Court made orders for the father to have unsupervised time with [X], that there should be an order for family therapy to occur. Joy Slattery disagrees and says that family therapy would be retraumatising for the mother.
The experts agreed that if Court decided to progress to unsupervised there should be a slow and gradual progression over time, not just weeks but months.
On 13 March 2026 the experts appeared in this trial to give concurrent evidence. Set out below is a distillation of the process that was followed. I have not recorded what each expert said because that will form the basis of my considerations for the determination of the parenting applications brought by the mother on the one hand and by the father on the other.

THE PROCEDURE FOR THE EXPERTS GIVING CONCURRENT EVIDENCE

At 10 am on 13 February 2026 the trial was resumed on day nine. The four experts had assembled in the well of the court. Counsel occupied their positions at the Bar table. I announced how the hot tub would unfold. In précis form, I announced the following in open court –
(a) the list of issues prepared following the conclave of experts would be marked as an agreed exhibit;

(b) after explaining the process to be adopted for the giving of the experts concurrent evidence, each barrister in the case would yield her and his place at the bar table facing the bench and each would thereafter sit at the bar table opposite the experts, occupying the place at the bar table ordinally occupied (at least in Victoria) by instructing solicitors;

(c) the first expert would be sworn or affirmed, and once sworn or affirmed counsel for the ICL would then ask the first expert to adopt her report or reports plus any accompanying affidavit after which the first expert would leave the witness box to take her place at the bar table;

(d) the second expert would then be sworn or affirmed and that once sworn or affirmed counsel for the ICL would ask that the second expert to adopt his report or reports plus any accompanying affidavit after which the second witness would leave the witness box to take his place at the bar table;

(e) the third expert would then be sworn or affirmed and that once sworn or affirmed counsel for the ICL would ask the third expert to adopt his report or reports plus an accompanying affidavit after which the third expert would leave the witness box to take his place at the bar table;

(f) the fourth expert would then be sworn or affirmed and that once sworn or affirmed counsel for the ICL would ask the fourth expert to adopt his report or reports plus an accompanying affidavit after which the fourth expert would leave the witness box to take his place at the bar table;

(g) the first expert would then be invited to state in précis form the substance of her evidence in this litigation placing (if thought desirable) such emphasis as may be thought relevant on the responses to all or any of the matters on the list of issues;

(h) after the first expert witness’s opening statement, the second expert would be asked whether and if so what he wished to say by way of agreement or disagreement with anything the first expert witness said by way of opening statement and the second expert witness would be invited to question the first expert witness about anything said by the first expert witness in her opening statement;

(i) the third expert witness would be asked whether and if so what he wished to say by way of agreement or disagreement with anything the first expert witness said by way of opening statement and the third expert witness would be invited to question the first expert witness about anything said by the first expert witness in her opening statement;

(j) the fourth expert witness would be asked whether and if so what he wished to say by way of agreement of disagreement with anything the first expert witness said by way of opening statement and the fourth expert witness would be invited to question the first expert witness about anything said by the first expert witness in her opening statement;

(k) counsel for the ICL would be invited to question the first expert witness;

(l) counsel for the mother would next be invited to question the first expert witness;

(m) counsel for the father would then be invited to question the first expert witness;

(n) the second expert witness would then be invited to state in précis form the substance of his evidence in this litigation placing (if thought desirable) such emphasis as may be thought relevant on responses to all or any of the matters on the list of issues;

(o) the second expert witness would be questioned by the first, third and fourth experts and by all counsel in the manner outlined already;

(p) the third expert witness would then provide an opening statement and then would be questioned by the first, second and third expert witness along with all counsel in the manner outlined above;

(q) the fourth expert witness would then provide an opening statement and he would be questioned by the first, second and third expert witnesses and by all counsel in the manner outlined above; and

(r) all experts would then be at liberty to raise any point favourable to that expert’s contentions with any other expert.

I invited all counsel to express their agreement or disagreement with any aspect of the procedure for the conduct of the experts’ concurrent evidence just narrated. All counsel agreed with the procedure outlined immediately above. No counsel expressed disagreement about any aspect of the way in which the experts would give their evidence concurrently.
The first expert was sworn and she adopted her reports. Counsel and all other experts had the opportunity to question her.
The second expert was sworn and he adopted his reports. Counsel and all other experts had the opportunity to question him.
The third expert was sworn and he adopted his reports. Counsel and all other experts had the opportunity to question him.
The fourth expert was sworn and he adopted his reports. Counsel and all other experts had the opportunity to question him.
All experts were invited to make any comment by way of final say so.
The hot tub concluded by the luncheon adjournment on 13 March 2026. The entirety of the experts’ evidence was concluded in one stage within three hours, thereby demonstrating the efficiency of the hot tub process.
